coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorimetric sensing strategy for highly sensitive detection of phytic acid and hydrogen peroxide
coredata.dc:creator: Qu, Zhengyi
Na, Weidan
Nie, Yixin
Su, Xingguang
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, we developed a sensitive sensor for phytic acid (PA) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) detection based on glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs@GSH). The fluorescence of GQDs@GSH was found to be effectively quenched by Fe3+ ions via photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process. Upon the addition of PA to GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ system, the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH was significantly restored due to the strong chelating and reducing ability of PA, Fe3+ ions could be reduced to Fe2+ ions by PA and formed PA/Fe2+ complex. Therefore, the “off–on” fluorescence method was constructed to detect PA by using GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ as a fluorescent probe. Furthermore, the method can be used for the detection of H2O2. H2O2 can destroy the chelate structure of PA/Fe2+, release Fe2+ ions and oxidize Fe2+ ions to produce Fe3+ ions, leading to the fluorescence quenching of GQDs@GSH again. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence sensing platform showed good linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity I/I0 and the concentration of PA and H2O2 in the range of 0.05–3 μmol L−1 and 0.5–10 μmol L−1, respectively. The detection limits of PA and H2O2 were 14 nmol L−1 and 0.134 μmol L−1, respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescence assay method was also applied in real sample analysis and satisfactory results were obtained.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence
Photo-induced electron transfer
Phytic acid
Hydrogen peroxide

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quenching experiments induced by Fe3+ ions
The design strategy for PA and H2O2 detection
Detection of PA and H2O2 in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The TEM image of GQDs@GSH (B) The size distribution of GQDs@GSH; (C) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs@GSH; The inset of (C) is the images of GQDs@GSH solution taken under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right); (D) The XPS spectra of GQDs@GSH.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs@GSH, GQDs@GSH/PA, GQDs@GSH/H2O2, GQDs@GSH/Fe3+, GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA and GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA/H2O2 system; (B) The fluorescence decay curves of GQDs@GSH and GQDs@GSH after the addition of Fe3+; Fe3+ and PA; (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of GQDs@GSH with different concentrations of Fe3+ (0, 2.5, 5, 12.5, 20, 25, 30, 35 μmol L−1); (D) Effect of Fe3+ concentration on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH.

(A) Effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ (30 μmol L−1) system; (B) Effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ (30 μmol L−1)/PA (3 μmol L−1) system; (C) Effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ (30 μmol L−1)/PA (3 μmol L−1)/H2O2 (10 μmol L−1) system; (D) Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH (black squares), GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ (30 μmol L−1) (red dots),GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ (30 μmol L−1)/PA (3 μmol L−1) (blue triangles) and GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ (30 μmol L−1)/PA (3 μmol L−1)/H2O2 (10 μmol L−1) (green triangles). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the GQDs@GSH (5 mg L−1)/Fe3+ (30 μmol L−1)/PA system with different concentrations of PA (0, 0.05, 0.25,0.45, 1.0, 2.0 and 3.0 μmol L−1) in pH 7.4 buffer solution; (B) Plot of the relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) of the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA system against the PA concentration, where I0 and I stand for the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ system in the absence and presence of PA.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the GQDs@GSH (5 mg L−1)/Fe3+ (30 μmol L−1)/PA (3 μmol L−1)/H2O2 system with different concentrations of H2O2 (0, 0.5, 1.5, 2.5, 5.0, 7.5 and 10 μmol L−1) in pH 7.4 buffer solution; (B) Plot of the relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) of the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA/H2O2 system against the concentration of H2O2, where I0 and I stand for the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA system in the absence and presence of H2O2.

(A) The fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ system with the interfering substances (20 μmol L−1 SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cl−, Cu2+, Hg2+, Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, cholesterol (CHO), glucose, lysine, threonine, tryptophan and phenylalanine); (B) The fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA system with the interfering substances (800 μmol l− 1 Na+ and Cl−, 100 μmol L−1 SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cu2+, Hg2+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, cholesterol (CHO), glucose, lysine, threonine, tryptophan and phenylalanine, 20 μmol L−1 human serum albumin (HSA), pepsin, trypsin and lysozyme).
Schematic illustration of the novel nanosensor for PA and H2O2 detection.

head.ce:title: A novel fluorimetric sensing strategy for highly sensitive detection of phytic acid and hydrogen peroxide

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, College of Chemistry, Jilin University, Changchun, 130012, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, we developed a sensitive sensor for phytic acid (PA) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) detection based on glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs@GSH). The fluorescence of GQDs@GSH was found to be effectively quenched by Fe3+ ions via photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process. Upon the addition of PA to GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ system, the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH was significantly restored due to the strong chelating and reducing ability of PA, Fe3+ ions could be reduced to Fe2+ ions by PA and formed PA/Fe2+ complex. Therefore, the “off–on” fluorescence method was constructed to detect PA by using GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ as a fluorescent probe. Furthermore, the method can be used for the detection of H2O2. H2O2 can destroy the chelate structure of PA/Fe2+, release Fe2+ ions and oxidize Fe2+ ions to produce Fe3+ ions, leading to the fluorescence quenching of GQDs@GSH again. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence sensing platform showed good linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity I/I0 and the concentration of PA and H2O2 in the range of 0.05–3 μmol L−1 and 0.5–10 μmol L−1, respectively. The detection limits of PA and H2O2 were 14 nmol L−1 and 0.134 μmol L−1, respectively. Furthermore, the fluorescence assay method was also applied in real sample analysis and satisfactory results were obtained.

ce:section.ce:para: Phytic acid (PA), also known as inositol hexaphosphate, is widely distributed in the cereal grains as the principal storage form for phosphorus [1]. And PA has a strong reducing power as a natural antioxidant product. Under physiological conditions, PA can form insoluble complexes with various metal cations in food, such as calcium, iron, zinc, etc., thereby inhibiting their absorption and utilization in the intestine [2,3]. The solubility of the complex formed under physiological conditions is very low, which may hinder the bioavailability of essential minerals in the human body and thus lead to insufficient absorption [4]. Therefore, people have long thought that it is an anti-nutrient material, and related research has also caused people's great interest in human nutrition [5]. Nevertheless, recently it is reported that PA also has various benefits for human health because it can reduce the risk of diabetes, cancer, heart disease and kidney stones [6,7].

In order to understand the role of PA accurately and provide accurate and useful information for its intake and metabolism, it is usually necessary to detect PA in the fields of biology, food and medicine science. Analytical methods have been reported in literature for measuring PA including pair chromatography and high-performance liquid chromatography [8,9]. Although all the above methods can quantitatively detect PA, there are some drawbacks, such as complex preparation of test samples, expensive equipment and poor sensitivity. Fluorescent sensing has the advantages of simple operation, high sensitivity and low cost. And optical sensing strategies have been highlighted because they are fast, continuous, and happen in real time.

Currently, there are some fluorescence sensing strategies for detecting PA, but the fluorescent probes used were mainly concentrated on organic dyes [5,10]. Compared with conventional organic dyes, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess unique optical properties including low toxicity, high biocompatibility and better stability [11,12]. With these attractive advantages of GQDs, many applications have been developed for chem/biosensing and bioimaging platforms based on GQDs [13–15].

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) is widely used in the fields of pharmaceutical, industrial, biological and environmental analysis because of its strong oxidizing capacity [16]. In addition, H2O2 is a catalytic byproduct of a great quantity of oxidation reactions, resulting in the release of the compound into water and air, causing serious environmental disruption [17]. However, in the living organism, H2O2 is a common marker and signal, the outbreak of H2O2 could affect various diseases, including cardiovascular, diabetes, cancer and neurodegenerative diseases [18]. Therefore, sensitive determination of H2O2 is one of the most important research questions in the field of chemistry and biology [19]. The development of sensors and biosensors for reliable detection of H2O2 has attracted a lot of attention [20–23].

In this paper, we developed a glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs@GSH)-based sensor for the detection of PA and H2O2. Scheme 1 illustrated the strategy of GQDs@GSH-based sensing platform for PA and H2O2 detection. Fe3+ ions can quench the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH efficiently through the coordination interactions between Fe3+ ions and carboxyl or aromatic hydroxyl groups on the surface of GQDs@GSH. PA has strong reducibility, it could reduce Fe3+ ions to produce Fe2+ ions. And the structure of PA (Fig. S1) had a high density of negatively charged phosphate groups on the surface, so that the generated Fe2+ ions could preferentially and selectively bind with PA. Therefore it could effectively restrain the quenching effect of Fe3+ ions on the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH. However, by adding a strong oxidative H2O2, it can destroy the chelate structure of PA/Fe2+ complex, release Fe2+ ions and oxidize Fe2+ ions to produce Fe3+ ions, leading to fluorescence quenching of GQDs@GSH again. So the fluorescent sensing method for sensitive and selective detection of PA and H2O2 was constructed.

Citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O),glutathione, phytic acid, hydrochloric acid and acetonitrile were purchased from Beijing Chemical Works. 2-amino-2-hydroxymethyl-1,3-propanediol (Tris) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were purchased from Beijing Dingguo Biotechnology Co., Ltd. K2SO4, KNO3, KCl, FeCl3, CuCl2, MnCl2, PbCl2, HgCl2, ZnCl2, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2· 6H2O, cholesterol, glucose, lysine, threonine, tryptophan and phenylalanine were purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Institute of Fine Chemicals. Pepsin, trypsin, human serum albumin (HSA) and lysozyme were purchased from Sino-American Biotechnology Co. Ltd. All chemicals used were of analytical reagent grade. The distilled water used throughout this study had a resistivity higher than 18 MΩ cm−1. The Tris–HCl buffer solution (0.1 mol L−1) was used as a medium for the detection process.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) experiment was tested on a Philips Tecnai F20 TEM operating. Fluorescence spectra were collected by a Shimadzu RF-5301 PC spectrofluorophotometer. UV–vis absorbance measurement was carried out on a Varian GBC Cintra 10e UV–visible Spectrophotometer. The fluorescence quantum yield of GQDs@GSH was measured on Edinburgh FLS920 by using an integrating sphere. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on an ESCALAB MK II X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. All pH measurements were made with a PHS-3C pH meter.

GQDs@GSH were synthesized based on a modified synthetic method in the literature [24]. Typically, 0.5 g of citric acid and 0.15 g of glutathione were mixed together, then the mixture was placed in a 5 mL beaker and heated to 260 °C. After about 3 min, the mixture was liquefied, the color of the solution changed from colorless to light yellow, and then turned brown within 10 min. Subsequently, the liquid was dissolved in 50 mL of ultrapure water and dialyzed for 10 h. Finally, the pure GQDs@GSH were obtained. The obtained GQDs@GSH showed stable blue fluorescence with a fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of 31.6%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quenching experiments induced by Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para: For fluorescence quenching experiment, various concentrations of ferric chloride solution and pH 7.4 Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.01 mol L−1) were mixed with 5 mg L−1 GQDs@GSH in a series of 2.0 mL calibrated test tubes. Then the mixture was diluted to 2.0 mL with deionized water. The fluorescence spectra were collected after shaking for 1 min at room temperature.

For PA detection, 30 μmol L−1 ferric chloride solution and pH 7.4 Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.01 mol L−1) were mixed with 5 mg L−1 GQDs@GSH in a series of 2.0 mL calibrated test tubes. Subsequently, various concentrations of PA solution were added to the resulting mixture. Then the mixture was diluted to 2.0 mL with deionized water. The fluorescence spectra were collected after shaking for 6 min at room temperature.

30 μmol L−1 ferric chloride solution and pH 7.4 Tris-HCl buffer solution (0.01 mol L−1) were mixed with 200 μL GQDs@GSH in a series of 2.0 mL calibrated test tubes. Then, 3 μmol L-1 PA solution was added to the resulting mixture and the mixture was shaken thoroughly for 6 min. Subsequently, different concentrations of H2O2 were introduced into the mixture. Then the mixture was diluted to 2.0 mL with deionized water. The fluorescence spectra were collected after shaking for 40 min at room temperature.

To prepare the corn samples, the corn grains were collected from local agricultural product markets and processed according to the previously reported procedure [25]. Briefly, a 0.5 g amount of fine-ground corn grains was extracted with 10 mL of 0.5 M HCl for 2 h at room temperature. The suspension was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 5 min and then the supernatant was purified by anionic-exchange treatment using a 0.7 M NaCl solution as eluent. The solution containing PA was adjusted to neutral pH and then diluted with deionized water to a 200 mL volumetric flask. The concentrations of PA were detected by following the same procedure described in section 2.5.

For serum samples detection, drug-free human blood samples were collected from healthy volunteer at the Hospital of Changchun China-Japan Union Hospital. All experiments were performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines, and the writing of informed consent for all samples was obtained from human subjects. All the blood samples were obtained through venipuncture and centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min after standing for 2 h at room temperature. The supernatant was separated and deproteinized by adding acetonitrile (CH3CN) to the samples (CH3CN: serum = 1:1). After vigorous shaking for 2 min, the mixture was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C. The obtained supernatant was filtered twice. The filtrate was adjusted to neutral pH and then diluted 200-fold with deionized water. Different concentrations of H2O2 were added to the diluted serum samples to prepare spiked samples. Real sample analysis was carried out using the procedure described above.

In this work, GQDs@GSH were employed as fluorescent probe to detect PA. The optical characteristics of GQDs@GSH were systematically characterized by employing transmission electron microscopy (TEM), fluorescence spectra and UV–vis spectroscopy, which were showed in Fig. 1 . As shown in Fig. 1A–B, GQDs@GSH were well distributed, showing a spherical morphology with an average diameter of 6.8 nm. Fig. 1C showed the GQDs@GSH exhibited the maximal emission at 430 nm (black line) and the strong absorbance at 353 nm (red line). Inset in Fig. 1C showed the GQDs@GSH solution was colorless under visible light and exhibited a strong blue under ultraviolet irradiation (365 nm), further indicating the blue fluorescence properties of GQDs@GSH. The chemical and elemental compositions of GQDs@GSH surface were further characterized by XPS. As shown in Fig. 1D, four main peaks at 283, 400, 532 and 166 eV (ascribed to C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p respectively) demonstrated the existence of C, N, O and S elements in GQDs@GSH.

The use of GSH in synthesis of GQDs has two advantages: increasing fluorescence quantum yield and improving biocompatibility of GQDs. The quantum yield of GQDs@GSH was measured to be 31.6%. The high fluorescence quantum yield may be attributed to the amination reaction between the carboxyl and epoxy groups on the surface of GQDs and the amine group of GSH. Carboxylic and epoxy groups were commonly used as non-radiative electron-hole complex centers, resulting in the non-emission properties of GQDs. When the amination reaction happened, the number of non-radiative electron–hole complex centers was reduced, thereby increasing the effective emission [26,27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The design strategy for PA and H2O2 detection
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the feasibility of this proposed sensing system for PA and H2O2 detection, GQDs@GSH were mixed with different reactants. As shown in Fig. 2 A, after the addition of Fe3+ ions, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH decreased greatly. There was no significant change in the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH after mixing with PA and H2O2, respectively. After addition of PA to GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ system, Fe3+ ions could be effectively reduced to Fe2+ ions and Fe2+ ions complexed with PA, leading to the distinct recovery of the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH. Subsequently, H2O2 destroyed the structure of PA/Fe2+ complex and oxidized Fe2+ to Fe3+ ions, leading to the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH decreased again.

The coordination interaction between Fe3+ ions and the carboxyl or aromatic hydroxyl groups on the surface of the GQDs@GSH lead to the formation of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ complex, and the quenching effect may be mainly caused by the effective electron transfer that occurred from GQDs@GSH to Fe3+ ions. When photo-excitation, GQDs@GSH excited state could release its energy in no electron transfer way. In this way, the excited state of electronic entered the unfilled d orbit of Fe3+, which led to the fluorescence quenching of GQDs@GSH through photo-induced electron transfer (PET) process. Previous studies have demonstrated that the surface state has a significant impact on the band gap and excited states of QDs, thus resulting in the change in fluorescence emission and lifetime [28,29]. To further confirm the quenching mechanism caused by Fe3+ ions, we measured the fluorescence decays to investigate the excited states of the GQDs@GSH in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions (Fig. 2B). The related data were given in Table S1. The fluorescence decay analysis showed the fluorescence lifetime of GQDs@GSH (0.89 ns) was longer than that of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ (0.447 ns), implying that Fe3+ ions quenched the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH through dynamic quenching process. And the fluorescence lifetime of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA mixture was recovered to 0.885 ns, which was close to that of GQDs@GSH alone (0.89 ns). This result indicated that the introduction of PA allowed the release of Fe3+ ions from the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ complex due to its affinity for PA was higher than that for GQDs @GSH.

We studied the effect of Fe3+ ions on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH. As displayed in Fig. 2C, a gradual decrease of the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH was observed with the increasing concentration of Fe3+ ions in the range from 0 to 30 μmol L−1. And then the fluorescence remained stable with further increasing concentration of Fe3+ ions to 35 μmol L−1 (Fig. 2D). Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of the GQDs@GSH could be modulated by Fe3+ ions. And 30 μmol L−1 Fe3+ was selected for the subsequent experiments. We also studied the effect of other metal ions including Cu2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Fe2+, Ca2+ and Pb2+ on the fluorescence response of GQDs@GSH. As shown in Fig. S2, GQDs@GSH can be effectively quenched by Fe3+ ions with high selectivity, while other ions had almost no effect on GQDs@GSH. The results indicated that GQDs@GSH exhibited a highly selective response towards Fe3+ ions. This provided a good basis for us to selectively detect PA.

In order to construct a fluorescence sensor with good sensing performance, we further optimized the detection conditions. In this work, the incubation times were investigated firstly. As shown in Fig. 3 A, when Fe3+ ions were added to GQDs@GSH solution, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH decreased immediately and reached a balance within 1 min. Fig. 3B showed the fluorescence recovery of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ system in the presence of PA as a function of incubation time. It could be seen that the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH increased gradually within 6 min and reached a plateau, indicating the interaction between Fe3+ ions and PA reached balance. The effect of incubation time on the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA/H2O2 system was studied in Fig. 3C. It could be seen that the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH increased to the maximum in 40 min incubation time and kept unchangeable with further increase in reaction time. The effect of pH was also studied. As shown in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH and GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ system almost remained stable in the pH range of 5.8–9.0. The maximum difference in fluorescence intensity between GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA and GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA/H2O2 system was occurred at pH = 7.4. Therefore, pH 7.4 was chosen in the following experiments.

The fluorescence spectra of the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA system with different concentrations of PA were shown in Fig. 4 . It could be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the system was gradually restored with the increasing concentration of PA. A good linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity I/I0 and PA concentration was obtained in the range of 0.05–3.0 μmol L−1 with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.997 (Fig. 4B). The linear regression equation was I/I0 = 0.984 + 1.632 [PA], μmol L−1, the detection limit (LOD) was 14 nmol L−1.

The fluorescence spectra of the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA/H2O2 system with different concentrations of H2O2 were shown in Fig. 5 A. It could be seen that the fluorescence intensity of the system gradually decreased with the increase of H2O2 concentration. Fig. 5B showed a good linear relationship between relative fluorescence intensity I/I0 and H2O2 concentration was obtained in the range from 0.5 to 10 μmol L−1 with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.998. The linear regression equation was: I/I0 = 0.985–0.059 [H2O2], μmol L−1, with the detection limit of 0.134 μmol L−1.

In addition, a comparison between this method and other methods published in the literature for PA and H2O2 determination in linear range and detection limit was listed in Table S2 and Table S3. Compared with other assay methods, the method we established provided a comparable or superior detection limit and linear range.

Selectivity is an important factor for evaluating the performance of a new detection method, especially for those with potential applications value in biomedical samples, a high selective response to the target over other potential interference substances is necessary. To investigate the specificity of the sensor for PA and H2O2 detection, its selectivity was further assessed by adding various interfering inorganic salts, metal ions and biomolecules to system under the same conditions. As shown in Fig. 6 A–B, after the addition of these interference substances, the fluorescence intensity of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ and GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA systems remained nearly constant and only after the addition of 1 μmol L-1 PA or 5 μmol L−1 H2O2, the fluorescence intensity showed obvious increase or decrease. The results indicated that the sensing platform showed the high selectivity to PA or H2O2. We further investigated the signal response of GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ and GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA system to PA and H2O2 in the presence of foreign substances, respectively. It could be seen from Fig. 6A–B (gray column) that even in the presence of the interferences, the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+ and GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA system still work the same for PA and H2O2 recognition, indicating that the proposed PA and H2O2 sensing system could resist interference from common inorganic salts, metal ions and biological species. Thus, the established sensor was suitable for selective detection of PA and H2O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of PA and H2O2 in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the feasibility of the proposed detection method in actual sample analysis, the proposed fluorescence method was used to detect PA and H2O2 in corn grain and human serum samples, respectively. The results obtained by a standard addition method were shown in Table S4-5. The UV-spectrophotometry method based on the quantitative reaction of PA with ferric iron and the color reaction of excess iron with sulfosalicylic acid, a standard method for PA determination in food was used as a contrast method. The proposed method for H2O2 determination in human serum samples was also compared to electrochemical method [30]. As can be seen in Table S4-5, a good agreement can be observed between the proposed assay and other method, demonstrating the accuracy of our method for PA and H2O2 determination. It could also be observed that the recoveries of PA in corn grain samples ranged from 94% to 105%, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was lower than 3.56. The recoveries of H2O2 were in the range of 96%–103% and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was lower than 3.07, which indicating that the proposed fluorimetric sensing strategy is feasible for the quantitative detection of PA and H2O2 in real samples.

In this paper, we have successfully developed a novel fluorescence sensing platform for PA and H2O2 detection based on GQDs@GSH. The fluorescence of GQDs@GSH quenched by Fe3+ ions was restored by PA due to the strong chelating and reducing ability of PA. Furthermore, upon addition of strong oxidizer H2O2 to the GQDs@GSH/Fe3+/PA system, the fluorescence of GQDs@GSH was quenched again. Moreover, this method was successfully applied in real sample determination with satisfactory results, indicating that the proposed method had potential application value in real sample determination of PA and H2O2.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775052 and No. 21575048), the Science and Technology Development project of Jilin province, China (No.20180414013GH).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Revised Supporting information Revised Supporting information

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.07.030.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Determination of phytic acid in cereals - a brief review
Food phytate and its hydrolysis products
Interaction of myo-inositol hexakisphosphate with alkali and alkaline earth metal ions: spectroscopic, potentiometric and theoretical studies
Synchronous fluorescence analysis of phytate in food
Determination of phytic acid in wheat and wheat products by reverse phase high performance liquid chromatography
Determination of phytic acid in urine by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry
Evaluation of phytic acid content of some tea and nut products by reverse-phase high performance liquid chromatography/visible detector
Synchronous fluorescence determination of phytic acid in foodstuffs and urine based on replacement reaction
Sensors and biosensors in support of EU Directives
Single-exciton optical gain in semiconductor nanocrystals
Rapid and simple nanosensor by combination of graphene quantum dots and enzymatic inhibition mechanisms
Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot for direct fluorescence detection of Al3+ in aqueous media and living cells
Amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots based ratiometric fluorescent nanosensor for ultrasensitive and highly selective recognition of horseradish peroxidase
Novel porous gold-palladium nanoalloy network-supported graphene as an advanced catalyst for non-enzymatic hydrogen peroxide sensing
A novel water-soluble sulfonated porphyrin fluorescence sensor for sensitive assays of H2O2 and glucose
Design of a sensing platform with dual performance for detection of hydrogen peroxide and Fe3+ based on a new fluorescent oligo N-phenylpyrrole derivative
Three-dimensional Fe- and N-incorporated carbon structures as peroxidase mimics for fluorescence detection of hydrogen peroxide and glucose
Fe-Co bimetallic alloy nanoparticles as a highly active peroxidase mimetic and its application in biosensing
1,4-Benzenediboronic-Acid-Induced aggregation of gold nanoparticles: application to hydrogen peroxide detection and biotin avidin-mediated immunoassay with naked-eye detection
A ratiometric fluorescence universal platform based on N, Cu codoped carbon dots to detect metabolites participating in H2O2-Generation reactions
Rational designed benzochalcone-based fluorescent probe for molecular imaging of hydrogen peroxide in live cells and tissues
Glutathione-functionalized graphene quantum dots as selective fluorescent probes for phosphate-containing metabolites
A carbon dot-based “off-on” fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of phytic acid
Highly efficient photoluminescent graphene oxide with tunable surface properties
Optically tunable amino-functionalized graphene quantum dots
Enhancing the luminescence of carbon dots with a reduction pathway
Electrochemical tuning of luminescent carbon nanodots: from preparation to luminescence mechanism
Fabrication of novel electrochemical biosensor based on graphene nanohybrid to detect H2O2 released from living cells with ultrahigh performance
